---
title: 'What Everybody Ought to Know About Strong Passwords!'
date: '2014-02-16T07:00:00.0000000+00:00'
draft: true
blogger_blog: 'Darrell Wolfe, Storyteller'
blogger_status: 'LIVE'
blogger_post_id: '983386396196912872'
blogger_filename: '/2014/02/what-everybody-ought-to-know-about.html'
blogger_source: 'takeout-20260223T235336Z-3-001.zip:Takeout/Blogger/Blogs/Darrell Wolfe, Storyteller/feed.atom'
categories:
  - 'Technology & Tools'
---

<h3>â€œYour Password Has Been Hacked!â€</h3><br />Youâ€™ve received the email. The email address is from someone you know. Itâ€™s your family or friend. But instead of a friendly greeting, or a pictureâ€¦ you get nothing but a link. <br /><br />Or â€œCheck this out: http://â€¦â€ followed by a link. <br /><br />How did these people get a hold of your friend or family members password? Usually itâ€™s because they used a weak password that was easy to break.<br /><br />But there are things you can do to make sure your password is not breakable. Or at least much more secure and probably not worth the effort.<br /><br /><a href="/images/imported/blogger-googleusercontent-com/9415848746-b33a9831d7-m14-4949d248c6e1.jpg"><img alt="Attribution Some rights reserved by Lulu Hoeller" border="0" src="https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjlJBHQaFGZ-_HyKevQW_vTzyVHExSZ1OuWlYML5vEsHvdRRsTDktA6HC3YSeTGv012rJO39dGM9F-YumTWRXslPJHcM5aqi0KG_u8gwdEKZc4bBaJwP1SRnC-578crbJ6TZFnnXJteLDQ/?imgmax=800" height="139" style="border-bottom-width: 0px; border-left-width: 0px; border-right-width: 0px; border-top-width: 0px; display: inline; margin: 5px 0px;" title="Attribution Some rights reserved by Lulu Hoeller" width="244" /></a> <br />Photo:&nbsp;<a href="http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0/">Some rights reserved</a> by <a href="http://www.flickr.com/photos/toaireisdivine/">Lulu Hoeller</a><br />&nbsp; <br /><h3>The Not-So-Secret To Strong Passwords</h3><br />Here are the keys to make your password as strong as it can be!<br /><br /><ul><li><strong>One each of the following at least, two each would be better. </strong></li></ul><ol>   <ol><li>CAPITOL LETTER </li><li>lower case letter </li><li>Number (1234567890) </li><li>Symbol/Character (! @ # $ % ^ &amp; * ( ) ) </li></ol></ol><br /><ul><li><strong>8 characters long for a normal strong password.</strong> </li></ul><blockquote>If you have particularly sensitive data you could choose a longer password and more combinations of the four categories above. </blockquote><br /><ul><li><strong>Not a word or name, as found in the English dictionary. </strong></li></ul><blockquote>Words can more easily searched by password breaking programs. One of the first things a password program might try to do is use words found in the dictionary.<br />Also, especially not a name of someone you know or love. </blockquote><h3>Pneumatic Devices and Other Memory Tricks</h3><br />Now hereâ€™s the trick, if you string together 8-12 random letters and symbols you arenâ€™t likely to remember it if you are an average person. Maybe a person with particularly good memory could. The rest of us need some memory tricks. <br /><br />So maybe you will pick yourself a word, phrase, or something else that will be easy to remember. However, now you edit and change. Use a number for a letter, leave a letter out, choose a symbol for a letter. <br /><br />So then, the word â€œredâ€ becomes:<br /><ul><li>R3d </li><li>r3D </li><li>Rd </li><li>rD </li></ul><br />And the word â€œandâ€ becomes:<br /><ul><li>Ad </li><li>@nD </li><li>@D </li><li>&amp; </li></ul>So choose things that work for you. â€œMyFathersâ€, could be:<br /><ul><li>M^f@TH3r5 </li></ul><br />While a long and truly random set of numbers, letter, and symbols would be the best and strongest password, I hope this provides for you some ideas that will help you use a password you remember but matches the password requirements that make it stronger. <br /><br />Now it's your turn.<br /><br /><br /><h4>Go practice on a Word Document or Notpad some password options that could work for you.&nbsp;</h4><ol><ol><li>Don't save these to a file because if someone got them it would be counter productive, but practice some passwords that could work.&nbsp;</li><li>Test them here:<a href="http://www.passwordmeter.com/" target="_blank">&nbsp;http://www.passwordmeter.com</a></li></ol></ol><div><br /></div><div>PS: Here's a link to another post by Printing By Design with some more ideas on creating good passwords: <a href="http://www.pbdink.com/blog/2012/06/09/youve-hacked-what/" target="_blank">Click Here</a></div><h4>Go leave a comment on the site. What other tricks do you know? What can you add to this conversation?&nbsp;</h4><br /><span style="background-color: white; font-family: 'Trebuchet MS', Trebuchet, sans-serif; font-size: 12.727272033691406px; line-height: 16.363636016845703px;">______________________________________________</span><br /><span style="background-color: white; font-family: 'Trebuchet MS', Trebuchet, sans-serif; font-size: 12.727272033691406px; line-height: 16.363636016845703px;"><br /></span><br /><div><br /><span style="background-color: white; font-family: 'Trebuchet MS', Trebuchet, sans-serif; font-size: 12.727272033691406px; line-height: 16.363636016845703px;">Published Â© Darrell G. Wolfe&nbsp;</span><br /><div><span style="background-color: white; font-family: 'Trebuchet MS', Trebuchet, sans-serif; font-size: 12.727272033691406px; line-height: 16.363636016845703px;"><br /></span></div><br /><ul><li><a href="http://www.darrellwolfe.com/" style="background-color: white; color: #397dbf; font-family: 'Trebuchet MS', Trebuchet, sans-serif; line-height: 16.363636016845703px; text-decoration: none;" target="_blank">http://www.DarrellWolfe.com/</a></li></ul><ul><li><a href="http://darrellwolfe.blogspot.com/2013/11/connect.html" style="background-color: white; color: #397dbf; font-family: 'Trebuchet MS', Trebuchet, sans-serif; line-height: 16.363636016845703px; text-decoration: none;">Connect</a><span style="background-color: white; font-family: 'Trebuchet MS', Trebuchet, sans-serif; line-height: 16.363636016845703px;">&nbsp;</span></li></ul><b>Disclosure of Material Connection: Some of the links in the post above are â€œaffiliate links.â€ This means if you click on the link and purchase the item, I will receive an affiliate commission. I may also have received one or more of the products or services mentioned above for free in the hope that I would mention it on my blog. Regardless, I only recommend products or services I use personally and believe will add value to my readers. I am disclosing this in accordance with the Federal Trade Commissionâ€™s&nbsp;<a href="http://www.gpo.gov/fdsys/pkg/CFR-2003-title16-vol1/content-detail.html" target="_blank">16 CFR, Part 255</a>: â€œGuides Concerning the Use of Endorsements and Testimonials in Advertising.â€ A Big Thank You to&nbsp;<a href="http://michaelhyatt.com/five-ways-to-comply-with-the-new-ftc-guidelines-for-bloggers.html" target="_blank">Michael Hyatt</a>&nbsp;for helping us Bloggers with these new legal disclosure requirements.</b><br /><div><b><br /></b></div></div><span style="background-color: white; font-family: 'Trebuchet MS', Trebuchet, sans-serif; font-size: 12.727272033691406px; line-height: 16.363636016845703px;"><br /></span>
